title: NOHARM (Numerous Options Harm Assessment for Risk in Medicine): A medical safety benchmark and randomized study of physician and AI teaming on clinical consultations
authors: David Wu, Fateme Nateghi Haredasht, Ethan Goh, Adam Rodman, Jonathan H. Chen, et al.
abstract: 1,100-task benchmark of primary care-to-specialist consultation cases measuring harmful errors from LLM-generated medical consultation recommendations. Covers 10 specialties, 12,747 expert annotations, 4,249 clinical management options. Tests 20 LLMs and 4 RAG clinical AI tools. Direct application carried potential for severe harm in up to 24.6% of cases. Errors of omission >80% of severe errors. Multi-agent AI teaming improved performance. Randomized study of 101 U.S.-licensed physicians showed AI assistance improved performance vs conventional resources, but AI-assisted physicians frequently omitted valuable AI recommendations.
relevance: Directly evaluates OpenEvidence and 3 other RAG clinical AI tools for clinical harm potential
key_findings: Stanford-Harvard ARISE free-choice arm revealed preference: physicians chose OpenEvidence in 22.3% of cases — more than ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, and all other external AI combined (19.8%)
24.6% of cases carried potential for severe harm from AI recommendations
Errors of omission accounted for >80% of severe errors
Clinical AI tools outperformed generalist LLMs
Multi-agent AI teaming further improved performance
AI-assisted physicians improved vs conventional resources
Combined human-AI responses would outperform both alone if AI recommendations were fully incorporated

title: Generalist Large Language Models Outperform Clinical Tools on Medical Benchmarks
abstract: Independent evaluation of two clinical AI tools (OpenEvidence and UpToDate Expert AI) against three frontier LLMs (GPT-5, Gemini 3 Pro, Claude Sonnet 4.5) using 1,000-item benchmark (MedQA + HealthBench). Generalist models consistently outperformed clinical tools. Published in Nature Medicine.
relevance: Head-to-head comparison of OpenEvidence and UpToDate against frontier generalist LLMs
key_findings: GPT-5 achieved highest scores across all stages
OpenEvidence and UpToDate showed deficits in completeness, communication quality, context awareness, and safety reasoning
Frontier LLMs outperformed specialized clinical tools on medical knowledge and clinician alignment

title: Expert Evaluation of Clinical AI Tools on Real Point-of-Care Clinical Queries (Real-POCQi)
abstract: Blinded evaluation built on 620 real-world Point-of-Care Queries (Real-POCQi) submitted to OpenEvidence by physicians across 30 specialties, plus 187 HealthBench questions. 149 physicians in specialty-matched head-to-head comparisons. OpenEvidence outperformed Claude Opus 4.8, Gemini 3.1 Pro, and GPT-5.5 on real-world clinical questions, contradicting the June Nature Medicine paper.
relevance: Directly contradicts the Nature Medicine study showing generalist LLMs beat clinical tools. Uses real-world queries from OpenEvidence platform.
key_findings: OpenEvidence outperformed Claude Opus 4.8, Gemini 3.1 Pro, and GPT-5.5 on real-world clinical questions
149 physicians in specialty-matched comparisons vs 12 clinicians in earlier study
Results contradict June 2026 Nature Medicine paper
Methodological distinction: head-to-head specialist matching vs isolated rubric scoring

title: A Novel Evaluation Benchmark for Medical LLMs: Illuminating Safety and Effectiveness in Clinical Domains (CSEDB)
abstract: Clinical Safety-Effectiveness Dual-Track Benchmark (CSEDB) — 30-criteria framework built on clinical expert consensus. 2,069 open-ended Q&A items across 26 clinical departments. Tested 6 LLMs: moderate overall performance (57.2%), safety 54.7%, effectiveness 62.3%. 13.3% performance drop in high-risk scenarios. Domain-specific medical LLMs outperformed general-purpose models.
relevance: Comprehensive safety+effectiveness benchmark for medical LLMs across 26 departments
key_findings: Average LLM performance only 57.2% — moderate, not ready for unsupervised clinical use
Safety scores (54.7%) lower than effectiveness (62.3%)
13.3% performance drop in high-risk scenarios
Domain-specific medical LLMs outperformed general-purpose models by 15.3% overall, 19.8% in safety
scale: 2,069 Q&A items, 26 clinical departments, 32 specialist physicians, 30 criteria

title: Beyond the Leaderboard: Rethinking Medical Benchmarks for Large Language Models (MedCheck)
abstract: Meta-evaluation of 56 medical LLM benchmarks using the MedCheck framework. Finds widespread systemic issues: disconnect from clinical practice, data integrity crises from contamination, and systematic neglect of safety-critical evaluation dimensions like model robustness and uncertainty awareness.
relevance: Evaluates the benchmark landscape itself — important context for interpreting all other benchmark results
key_findings: 75% of benchmarks focus on clinical workflow applications
Widespread data contamination risks unmitigated
Systematic neglect of safety-critical dimensions (robustness, uncertainty)
Profound disconnect from real clinical practice

title: LLMEval-Med: A Real-world Clinical Benchmark for Medical LLMs with Physician Validation
abstract: New benchmark covering 5 core medical areas with 2,996 questions from real-world EHRs and expert-designed clinical scenarios. Automated evaluation pipeline with LLM-as-Judge framework validated through human-machine agreement analysis.
relevance: Methodology advancement for real-world clinical LLM evaluation
key_findings: 2,996 questions from real EHRs and clinical scenarios
Automated LLM-as-Judge pipeline validated against expert scoring
Addresses limitations of multiple-choice-only benchmarks

title: Aligning Clinical Needs and AI Capabilities: A Survey on LLMs for Medical Reasoning
abstract: Survey establishing 5-level competency scheme (Miller's Pyramid) for medical LLMs. Links deductive, inductive, and abductive reasoning to clinical tasks. Benchmark with 18 models showing medical specialist models excel in diagnosis while general models lead in decision support and dialogue.
relevance: Framework for understanding different clinical AI capabilities across reasoning levels
key_findings: Medical specialist models excel in diagnosis-centric tasks
General models lead in decision support and dialogue
5-level competency scheme from knowledge recall to dynamic case management
18 models evaluated across reasoning levels

title: Towards Reliable Medical LLMs: Benchmarking and Enhancing Confidence Estimation in Medical Consultation
abstract: Evaluates confidence estimation in LLMs during clinical consultations. Existing benchmarks evaluate single-turn static settings; this study addresses multi-turn evidence accumulation. Finds significant gaps in model confidence calibration.
relevance: Addresses reliability and confidence calibration — critical for clinical deployment safety
key_findings: Existing benchmarks overlook confidence-correctness coupling in multi-turn consultations
Models show poor confidence calibration as evidence accumulates
Proposes methods for enhancing confidence estimation

title: Prescribing Caution: A Critique of OpenEvidence to Answer Medication-Related Questions
abstract: Critique of OpenEvidence's accuracy answering medication-related clinical questions. Identifies specific gaps and areas for improvement in pharmacotherapy recommendations.
relevance: Direct evaluation of OpenEvidence on medication-related questions
key_findings: Identified gaps in OpenEvidence medication recommendations

title: Large Language Models and Retrieval-Augmented Platforms for the Diagnosis and Management of Periodontal Diseases: A Blinded Expert-Rated Comparative Study of 11 Systems
abstract: Blinded expert-rated comparison of 11 LLM and RAG platforms for periodontal disease diagnosis and management. One of the largest head-to-head clinical AI comparisons in dentistry.
relevance: Broad comparison of 11 clinical AI systems including OpenEvidence
key_findings: 11 systems compared in blinded expert-rated study

title: AI-enabled clinical decision support in breast cancer care: a blinded multicenter benchmarking study comparing medically specialized with a general-purpose system
abstract: Blinded multicenter benchmarking comparing medically specialized AI systems against general-purpose systems in breast cancer clinical decision support.
relevance: Specialized vs general-purpose AI comparison in oncology
key_findings: Multicenter blinded evaluation in breast cancer care
tools_evaluated: Specialized medical AI vs general-purpose LLM

title: Large-Scale Evaluation of Five Large Language Models in Anesthesia Decision-Making
abstract: Large-scale evaluation of 5 LLMs on anesthesia decision-making tasks. Assesses clinical reasoning and safety in perioperative scenarios.
relevance: Evaluation of LLMs in anesthesia — specialty-specific benchmarking
key_findings: 5 LLMs evaluated on anesthesia decision-making

title: Evaluation of ChatGPT, Gemini, and OpenEvidence in Obstetric and Gynecologic Clinical Decision Scenarios
abstract: Head-to-head comparison of ChatGPT, Gemini, and OpenEvidence in OB/GYN clinical decision scenarios.
relevance: Direct comparison of OpenEvidence against ChatGPT and Gemini in OB/GYN
key_findings: Three major AI tools compared in OB/GYN scenarios

title: Assessment of OpenEvidence responses to AAOS clinical practice guidelines for ACL reconstruction
abstract: Assesses OpenEvidence's responses against AAOS clinical practice guidelines for ACL reconstruction. Measures adherence to established orthopedic guidelines.
relevance: Direct evaluation of OpenEvidence against established clinical guidelines
key_findings: Assesses OpenEvidence adherence to AAOS guidelines

title: Integrating AI into infection control: Evaluating the accuracy and consistency of four leading platforms across three regions
abstract: Evaluates accuracy and consistency of 4 leading AI platforms across 3 regions for infection control questions.
journal: J Assoc Med Microbiol Infect Dis Can
relevance: Cross-regional evaluation of clinical AI platforms in infectious disease
key_findings: 4 platforms evaluated across 3 geographic regions

title: Gut Instincts, Machine Decisions: Evaluating AI Accuracy in the Diagnosis and Treatment of Disorders of Gut-Brain Interaction
abstract: Evaluates AI accuracy in diagnosis and treatment of disorders of gut-brain interaction (DGBI).
relevance: Specialty-specific AI evaluation in gastroenterology
key_findings: Evaluates AI accuracy for DGBI diagnosis and treatment

title: Comparison of Large Language Model-Based Systems and Prompt Engineering for Internal Medicine Clinical Pharmacy Cases
abstract: Comparison of LLM-based systems and prompt engineering approaches for internal medicine clinical pharmacy cases.
relevance: Pharmacy-specific AI evaluation with prompt engineering comparison
key_findings: Compares LLM systems and prompt engineering for pharmacy cases

title: Reproducibility of AI-Generated Antibiotic Recommendations in Standardized Clinical Scenarios
abstract: Evaluates reproducibility of AI-generated antibiotic recommendations across standardized clinical scenarios. Tests consistency and reliability of clinical AI recommendations.
relevance: Reproducibility evaluation — critical for clinical AI reliability assessment
key_findings: Tests reproducibility of AI antibiotic recommendations

title: Evidence-Grounded AI for Musculoskeletal Care (OrthoPilot)
authors: Wenjie Li, Yujie Zhang, Fanrui Zhang, et al.
abstract: Clinical AI system (OrthoPilot) powered by LLM that integrates hospital data streams with authoritative external knowledge for continuous musculoskeletal management. Compared with 81 orthopaedic physicians, surpassed experts with 25 years of experience in diagnostic reasoning, clinical decision-making and management planning. Prospective study of 1,870 complex cases showed 10.6% increase in full-chain management success. 8-month randomised deployment of 8,240 inpatients increased cumulative cases per bed by 9.7%.
journal: arXiv preprint (cs.AI) [WITHDRAWN - authors request withdrawal pending consent]
relevance: Clinical AI system using LLM for longitudinal management with physician comparison benchmark
key_findings: OrthoPilot surpassed orthopaedic experts with 25 years of experience
10.6% increase in full-chain management success in prospective study
9.7% increase in cases per bed during 8-month randomised deployment
Benchmark from real-world EHRs spanning 1,000 disease codes
scale: 1,870 prospective cases, 8,240 inpatients, 81 physicians, 60 external clinical centres

title: Agentic systems for breast cancer treatment recommendations
authors: Vinicius Anjos de Almeida, Nicolas Henrique Borges, Leonardo Vicenzi, et al.
abstract: Evaluated agentic LLM systems for breast cancer treatment recommendation generation using 72 real clinical cases across stages I to IV and 1,147 case-specific rubrics. Seven pipelines compared including single-LLM baselines, tool-augmented systems, and multi-agent architectures. Best configuration (Claude Opus 4.8 with D&C+SA pipeline) achieved 0.594 global score. Oncologist-led error analysis revealed persistent clinically relevant failures including incorrect recommendations, citation errors, and overconfidence.
relevance: Evaluates agentic LLM systems for clinical decision support in oncology with rubric-based benchmarking
key_findings: Best system (Claude Opus 4.8 + D&C+SA) achieved 0.594 global score - insufficient for unsupervised use
Tool use and agent autonomy had mixed effects
Persistent failures: incorrect recommendations, flawed justifications, citation errors, overconfidence
Performance varied by clinical domain and disease stage

title: CLIR-Bench: Benchmarking Multimodal Question Answering over Irregular Clinical Time Series
authors: Frank Nie, Ethan B. Liu, Yuan Zhu, et al.
abstract: Benchmark for irregular clinical time series QA constructed from de-identified ICU records. Contains 6,600 QA instances spanning 11 clinical variables, organized into 4 capability dimensions and 11 tasks. Each question linked to explicit temporal evidence. Experiments show generalist models struggle to retrieve and reason over sparse clinical evidence.
relevance: Clinical AI benchmark for irregular time series QA - evaluates clinical decision support capabilities
key_findings: 6,600 QA instances from de-identified ICU records
Generalist models struggle with sparse clinical evidence retrieval
Evaluates both answer accuracy and evidence use
11 clinical variables, 4 capability dimensions, 11 tasks

title: The Large Cancer Assistant (LCA): A Model-Agnostic Orchestration Framework for Scalable Clinical Decision Support in Oncology
abstract: Model-agnostic, post-hoc orchestration framework for scalable clinical decision support in oncology. 7-tuple architecture grounded in Algorithmic Impermeability. Uses Geometric Deep Learning to standardize multimodal patient data. Cancer Switching Module orchestrates data. Proof of concept validated orchestration logic across four technical scenarios with 100% recall rate for failure-safety.
relevance: Clinical decision support orchestration framework for oncology - architectural approach to scalable clinical AI
key_findings: Model-agnostic design maintains invariant routing during AI model swaps
100% recall rate for generating Supplementary Data Requests under data anomalies
Structurally decouples multimodal ingestion from feature inference
Standardized Intermediate Payload enables EMR interoperability
scale: Proof of concept with 4 technical scenarios

title: Medi-Gemma: A Hybrid Clinical Decision Support System Integrating Deterministic EMR Analytics and Retrieval-Augmented Generation
authors: Mohammed Saim Ahmed Quadri, Yunzhe Xue, Justin W. Ady, Usman Roshan
abstract: Clinical Decision Support System for wound pathology triage and workflow automation. Decoupled framework separating clinical perception from data orchestration. Multi-stage pipeline with ClinicalOrchestrator, hierarchical IntentRouter, ClinicalRAGEngine using CPU-optimized vector store. Ground Truth Injection Module intercepts patient-specific queries. Safety compliance via deterministic ProtocolManager and SafetyVerifier phrase filter.
relevance: RAG-based clinical decision support system with deterministic safety safeguards - directly relevant to clinical AI tool evaluation
key_findings: Eliminates semantic context drift through Ground Truth Injection Module
Prevents database compilation crashes
Improves factual adherence to backend clinical repositories
Deterministic safety compliance via ProtocolManager and SafetyVerifier

title: MMIR-TCM: Memory-Integrated Multimodal Inference and Retrieval for TCM Clinical Decision Support
authors: Lihui Luo, Joongwon Chae, Ziyan Chen, et al.
abstract: Framework integrating multimodal LLM with memory-augmented segmentation and RAG for Traditional Chinese Medicine clinical decision support. Three-stage architecture: Memory-SAM for tongue extraction, fine-tuned Qwen3-VL for tongue diagnosis, Qwen3-based RAG for evidence-grounded clinical decision support. Outperforms GPT-4o and Gemini 2.5 Flash. Introduces TDEU domain-specific evaluation metric.
relevance: RAG-based clinical decision support system with multimodal integration - evaluates against frontier LLMs
key_findings: Outperforms GPT-4o and Gemini 2.5 Flash on TCM clinical tasks
Three-stage architecture with memory-augmented segmentation
Novel TDEU metric incorporating semantic understanding and diagnostic importance
Introduces MedTCM large-scale multimodal dataset

title: FaithMed: Training LLMs For Faithful Evidence-Based Medical Reasoning
authors: Zhiyun Zhang, Liwen Sun, Xiang Qian, Chenyan Xiong
abstract: Framework combining clinician-designed rubrics with reinforcement learning using step-level process reward assignment. Formalizes evidence-based medicine principles as process-level criteria. Improves over agentic-search baselines by 9% on average across 7 medical benchmarks. Raises evidence-based medicine rubric scores by 15.5% over baselines.
relevance: Medical LLM benchmark and training framework for evidence-based reasoning - evaluates faithfulness of clinical AI reasoning
key_findings: +9% improvement over agentic-search baselines across 7 medical benchmarks
+5.8% improvement over outcome-only RL
+15.5% improvement in evidence-based medicine rubric scores
Explicit step-level supervision improves both task success and reasoning faithfulness

title: Readable but Not Controllable: Neuron-Level Evidence for Medical LLM Hallucination
authors: Vijay Vankadaru, Asha Matthews, Tanya Roosta, Peyman Passban
abstract: Studies hallucination in medical LLMs at neuron level. Probe achieves AUROC 0.77-0.86 for detection. Hallucination signal is distributed and redundant. Sharp gap between decodability and controllability - same structure that makes hallucination detectable does not enable reliable neuron-level control. Suggests hallucination mitigation requires more than identifying right neurons.
relevance: Directly addresses clinical AI safety - hallucination detection and control in medical LLMs
key_findings: Probe reliably detects hallucination with AUROC 0.77-0.86
Hallucination signal is distributed and redundant, not narrowly localized
Sharp gap between decodability and controllability of hallucination
Neuron-level steering does not reliably correct hallucination
tools_evaluated: 4 open-source models across medical QA datasets
scale: 4 models, multiple medical QA datasets, 16 model-dataset combinations

title: IMCBench: A benchmark for multimodal LLMs in Image-grounded Medical Conversations
authors: Maria Xenochristou, Ashutosh Joshi, Korosh Vatanparvar, et al.
abstract: Image-grounded, multi-turn medical conversation benchmark pairing real clinical images with synthetic patient profiles. Evaluates safety, accuracy, and appropriate use of uncertainty. Benchmarks 8 multimodal frontier models across 4 model families. Claude Opus 4.6 achieves highest score (3.61). Safety degrades for malignant and rare conditions. Visual input and EHR context contribute to safe guidance.
relevance: Medical AI benchmark for multimodal clinical conversations - evaluates safety and accuracy across frontier models
key_findings: Claude Opus 4.6 highest overall (3.61/5)
Safety degrades for malignant (-0.27) and rare conditions (-0.27)
Visual input removal drops safety by 0.18, EHR context removal by 0.23
Accurate clinical description does not guarantee safe patient guidance
scale: 8 multimodal frontier models, clinical images with synthetic patient profiles

title: Clinical Harness for Governable Medical AI Skill Ecosystems
authors: Tianhan Xu, Lei Bao, Zhe Hu, Tian Shen, Yongxiang Wang
abstract: Runtime governance architecture for clinical AI skills. Registers, orchestrates, constrains, and monitors clinical AI skills. Uses osteoporosis as exemplar showing how knowledge-driven, data-driven, and physics-enhanced skills can support lifecycle care. Provides governed substrate for future medical agents.
relevance: Clinical AI governance and safety framework - addresses safe deployment of clinical AI skills
key_findings: Defines clinical AI skills as governed, persistent capabilities
Runtime governance architecture for registering, orchestrating, constraining AI skills
Osteoporosis exemplar demonstrates lifecycle care support
Provides substrate for future medical AI agents
scale: Framework paper with osteoporosis case study

title: MedBench v5: A Dynamic, Process-Oriented, and Hallucination-Aware Benchmark for Clinical Multimodal Models
authors: Jinru Ding, Chuchu Jiang, Lu Lu, et al.
abstract: Redesigned benchmark for clinical multimodal models moving from static QA to dynamic, process-oriented evaluation. Dual-dimensional framework: Clinical Cognitive Responsiveness (13 sub-dimensions) and Medical Atomic Skills (4 agent environments, 63 tasks). Three switchable stressors for factorized degradation analysis. Dynamic process audit protocol with 5 reasoning nodes. Hallucination propagation monitoring.
relevance: Comprehensive clinical AI benchmark with hallucination detection and process-oriented evaluation
key_findings: 63 tasks across 4 agent environments and 13 clinical sub-dimensions
Strong overall performance does not guarantee process stability
Stressors disrupt contradiction detection, diagnosis updating, hallucination propagation
Silent hallucination captured through 4 interaction stages
tools_evaluated: Frontier multimodal models (language, vision-language, agent systems)

title: MedRLM: Recursive Multimodal Health Intelligence for Long-Context Clinical Reasoning, Sensor-Guided Screening, Evidence-Grounded Decision Support, and Community-to-Tertiary Referral Optimization
abstract: Recursive Multimodal Health Intelligence framework for long-context clinical reasoning. Treats patient case as external clinical environment that can be recursively inspected, decomposed, retrieved, verified, and synthesized. Coordinates specialized agents for clinical text, longitudinal EHR, medical imaging, sensor signals, guideline retrieval, uncertainty auditing, and referral planning. Clinical Evidence Graph Memory connects patient observations with retrieved evidence.
relevance: RAG-based clinical decision support framework with multimodal reasoning and agent coordination
key_findings: Recursive inspection of patient cases as external clinical environment
Specialized agents for text, EHR, imaging, sensors, guidelines, uncertainty, referral
Clinical Evidence Graph Memory connects observations with evidence
Sensor-guided recursive triggering for abnormal physiological patterns
scale: Framework paper with evaluation design using public and credentialed datasets

title: Language Models as Interfaces, Not Oracles: A Hybrid LLM-ML System for Pediatric Appendicitis
abstract: Hybrid system (ClaMPAPP) using LLM as interface rather than decision-maker. Extracts schema-constrained features from narratives, passes to XGBoost classifier. Evaluated on two independent pediatric appendicitis cohorts. LLM-as-interface design outperformed end-to-end LLMs while minimizing missed appendicitis cases. End-to-end LLMs showed unstable sensitivity-specificity trade-offs.
relevance: Clinical decision support using LLM as interface for structured ML prediction - safety-focused design pattern
key_findings: LLM-as-interface, ML-as-predictor design outperforms end-to-end LLMs
Minimizes missed appendicitis cases (key safety concern in acute triage)
End-to-end LLMs showed unstable sensitivity-specificity trade-offs
Greater degradation under narrative reordering for end-to-end LLMs
scale: Two independent pediatric appendicitis cohorts from German hospitals

title: PhysAssistBench: Are LLMs Ready to Assist Physicians? Interactive Doctor-Patient-EHR Assistance
authors: Tianming Du, Peijie Yu, Sihan Shang, et al.
abstract: Benchmark for interactive doctor-patient-EHR assistance. Built from real MIMIC-IV cases with agentic patients that turn static EHR records into multi-turn clinical scenarios. 1,296 manually reviewed and physician-validated turns. Current models remain unreliable - reliable assistance requires coordination across knowledge, communication, and systems.
relevance: Physician-AI teaming benchmark - directly evaluates clinical AI assistant readiness for real-world clinical workflows
key_findings: Current models remain unreliable for interactive clinical assistance
Reliable assistance requires coordination across knowledge, communication, and systems
1,296 physician-validated turns from real MIMIC-IV cases
Exposes key bottleneck: coordination across capabilities, not isolated gains
scale: 1,296 manually reviewed and physician-validated turns, real MIMIC-IV cases

title: AIPatient Arena: EHR-grounded evaluation of large language models in end-to-end clinical consultation workflows
authors: Jiahui Niu, Huizi Yu, Wenkong Wang, et al.
abstract: EHR-grounded evaluation framework for LLMs across 8 dimensions of clinical competence. Integrates EHR data into patient-specific knowledge graphs for multi-turn physician-patient interactions. 437 patients in primary cohort, 119 and 67 in OOD validation cohorts. Persistent weaknesses in handling ambiguous responses, information coverage, and diagnostic accuracy. Process-based evaluation reveals recurrent interaction failures.
relevance: Clinical AI benchmark for end-to-end consultation workflows - evaluates clinical competence dimensions
key_findings: LLMs performed well in questioning skills and ethics (4.43-4.99/5)
Persistent weaknesses in handling ambiguity (2.57-3.32/5), information coverage (2.08-3.02/5), diagnostic accuracy (2.63-3.55/5)
Recurrent failures: repetitive questioning, omission of medical history, inadequate uncertainty handling
Final-answer accuracy alone is insufficient for evaluating clinical readiness
scale: 437 primary cohort patients, 119 + 67 OOD validation patients, 8 competence dimensions

title: Treatment Response Optimized Clinical Decision Support AI System via Digital Twin Simulation
authors: Xinyu Qin, Anil K. Sood, Ruiheng Yu, et al.
abstract: Online adaptive framework integrating Treatment Effect estimation, patient Digital Twin simulation, and Reinforcement Learning for sequential decision-making. Rule-based safety module blocks contraindicated treatments. Cases with model disagreement flagged for clinician review. Validated on synthetic simulator and real-world ovarian cancer dataset from TCGA. Superior effectiveness vs baselines with low latency.
relevance: Clinical decision support AI system with safety constraints and clinician-supervised design
key_findings: Superior effectiveness vs standard baselines in both simulated and clinical settings
Rule-based safety module blocks contraindicated treatments
Low latency with expert consultation needed for minority of cases
Continuous learning loop from practical use
tools_evaluated: Proposed CDSAS with Digital Twin + RL
scale: Synthetic clinical simulator + TCGA ovarian cancer dataset

title: Compositional Reasoning Depth Predicts Clinical AI Failure: Empirical Evidence Consistent with Transformer Compositionality Limits in EHR Question Answering
abstract: Introduces hop-count taxonomy as predictor of LLM failure on EHR QA. Questions requiring more inferential steps produce disproportionately more errors. All tested models show monotone accuracy decline with hop count. Extended thinking did not flatten accuracy-depth curve. Hop count is cross-architecture predictor of LLM error on EHR QA.
relevance: Clinical AI safety - predicts when clinical AI systems fail based on reasoning complexity, deployment risk stratification
key_findings: Monotone accuracy decline with hop count across all models (Claude, GPT-4o, GPT-5.4)
OR per hop: 0.72 (Claude), 0.58 (GPT-4o), 0.80 (GPT-5.4) - all statistically significant
Extended thinking did not significantly flatten accuracy-depth curve
Hop count is theory-motivated, cross-architecture predictor of clinical AI error
scale: 313 MedAlign EHR QA pairs, 4 hop levels, 3 models, 2 providers

title: MedFailBench: A Clinician-Built Open-Source Benchmark for Medical AI Safety Boundary Inspection
abstract: Most medical AI benchmarks measure whether a model knows the correct answer. MedFailBench asks a different question: which safety boundary failed? A clinician-built synthetic benchmark and failure atlas that labels medical AI errors by severity (1-5) and safety gate type: missed urgent escalation, unsafe remote dosing, unsafe discharge reassurance, evidence fabrication, unsafe protocol execution, source support gap. Contains 44 clinician-reviewed synthetic cases with severity annotations, a live HuggingFace leaderboard preview, a safety gate taxonomy, a clinical severity rubric, and an automated pipeline for archiving model-response screening runs. Released under Apache-2.0 and CC-BY-4.0.
relevance: Clinician-built benchmark for medical AI safety boundary inspection — directly relevant to clinical AI safety evaluation
key_findings: Introduces a safety gate taxonomy with 6 failure types: missed urgent escalation, unsafe remote dosing, unsafe discharge reassurance, evidence fabrication, unsafe protocol execution, source support gap
44 clinician-reviewed synthetic cases with severity annotations on a 1-5 scale
Open-source under Apache-2.0 and CC-BY-4.0 with Zenodo DOI
Live HuggingFace leaderboard preview
No patient data, clinical validation claims, or model rankings included in current release (v0.2.1)
tools_evaluated: Any medical AI system (framework is model-agnostic)
scale: 44 clinician-reviewed synthetic cases, 6 safety gate types, 5 severity levels

title: MamaBench: Benchmarking LLM Robustness in Maternal and Child Health Diagnosis through Counterfactual Clinical Perturbation
authors: Thanni Adewuyi, Anuoluwa Sotome, Samuel Okoko, Angel Ezendu, Oluwafunke Akinbuwa, Oluwaseun Odunsi, Oluwasegun Oguntuase, Oluwadarasimi Oguntuase, Ifeoma Nwabueze, Abiodun Adereni
abstract: First counterfactual benchmark for maternal and paediatric AI: 434 expert-authored clinical narratives in 217 pairs across 371 pathologies, evaluated via the Bias Trap Rate (BTR) — the conditional probability that a model fails the counterfactual given success on the base case. Proposes Evidence-Anchored RAG (EA-RAG), a three-stage retrieval method replacing aggregate similarity with an evidence coverage objective. Across eight configurations of four frontier LLMs, base accuracy overstates robust accuracy by 16-28 percentage points. EA-RAG achieves 20.3% BTR and 65.0% robust accuracy on Claude Sonnet 4.6, a 5.5 percentage point BTR reduction without degrading base accuracy. Residual 20% BTR confirms counterfactual robustness in clinical AI remains an open challenge.
relevance: Counterfactual benchmark for maternal/pediatric clinical AI with novel RAG approach — directly evaluates clinical AI decision support robustness
key_findings: Base accuracy overstates robust accuracy by 16-28 percentage points in every model tested
Introduces Bias Trap Rate (BTR) as a novel metric for counterfactual robustness in clinical AI
Evidence-Anchored RAG (EA-RAG) reduces BTR by 5.5 percentage points without degrading base accuracy
Residual 20% BTR confirms counterfactual robustness in clinical AI remains an open challenge
434 expert-authored clinical narratives in 217 pairs across 371 pathologies
tools_evaluated: 4 frontier LLMs (8 configurations), Claude Sonnet 4.6 with EA-RAG
scale: 434 clinical narratives, 217 counterfactual pairs, 371 pathologies, 4 LLMs

title: Guideline-anchored retrieval-augmented generation outperforms baseline and literature-only configurations in gynecologic oncology decision support: A pre-integration benchmark
authors: Dukes D, Yost C, Wang R, et al.
abstract: Pre-integration benchmark evaluating guideline-anchored RAG vs baseline and literature-only configurations in gynecologic oncology decision support. Demonstrates RAG with guideline anchoring outperforms alternatives.
relevance: Direct evaluation of RAG-based clinical decision support in gynecologic oncology - relevant to OpenEvidence's RAG architecture
key_findings: Guideline-anchored RAG outperforms baseline and literature-only configurations
Pre-integration benchmark for gynecologic oncology CDS

title: Clinician-Level Agreement Without Clinical Caution: LLM Evaluator Limits in Medical AI Benchmarking
authors: William Philipp, Finn Fassbender, Thorsten Langer, Martje Pauly, Rebecca Herzog, Alexander Baumann, Markus Hobert, Theresa Paulus, Ip Chi Wang, Lukas Goede, Johanna Reimer, Sebastian Löns, Ronald Böck, Sebastian Fudickar
abstract: Open-response evaluation provides stronger clinical validity than multiple-choice benchmarks but creates a scoring bottleneck that motivates automated LLM-as-a-Judge approaches. We introduce MedQADE, the first standardized open-response clinical benchmark for German, comprising 3,800 items annotated by ten practicing physicians and nine LLM evaluators. The top-performing evaluator (Gemini 3 Flash) reached alignment consistent with the physician ceiling (kappa=0.694 vs kappa=0.709), but automated evaluators exhibited near-absent clinical metacognition: physicians scaled abstention with item difficulty, while frontier models assigned definitive scores in every case. Systematic lineage-dependent biases found models preferentially scored architectural siblings.
relevance: Evaluates limits of LLM-as-judge approaches in medical AI benchmarking — critical context for interpreting all benchmark results using automated evaluators
key_findings: LLM evaluators can achieve statistical alignment with physician ceiling but lack clinical metacognition
Physicians scaled abstention with item difficulty; frontier models assigned definitive scores in every case
Systematic lineage-dependent biases: models preferentially scored architectural siblings
Statistical alignment does not ensure clinical caution
Evaluator independence requires explicit verification

title: Can LLMs Score Medical Diagnoses and Clinical Reasoning as well as Expert Panels?
abstract: Evaluating medical AI systems using expert clinician panels is costly and slow, motivating the use of LLMs as alternative adjudicators. We evaluate an LLM jury composed of three frontier AI models scoring 3,333 diagnoses on 300 real-world middle-income country (MIC) hospital cases. Results provide compelling evidence that a calibrated, multi-model LLM jury can serve as a trustworthy and reliable proxy for expert clinician evaluation in medical AI benchmarking.
relevance: Validates LLM jury approach as proxy for expert clinician evaluation in medical AI benchmarking — important for scaling benchmark evaluations
key_findings: Calibrated multi-model LLM jury can serve as trustworthy proxy for expert clinician evaluation
3,333 diagnoses scored on 300 real-world MIC hospital cases
Compelling evidence for reliability of LLM-based evaluation in medical AI

title: AI-based Clinical Decision Support for Primary Care: A Real-World Study
abstract: Real-world study of an LLM-based clinical decision support tool (AI Consult) at Penda Health clinics in Nairobi, Kenya. The system triggers asynchronously during clinical workflow decision points in the EMR. AI group clinicians saw significant reductions in clinical failure modes including incorrect primary diagnosis, inappropriate medications, missing patient education and follow-up plans, and missing history/investigation details. LLM evaluators found greater differences than physician evaluators (22% reduction in treatment errors, 19% in diagnostic errors).
relevance: Real-world evaluation of LLM-based CDS tool showing significant error reduction in live clinical setting — important evidence for CDS effectiveness
key_findings: Significant reductions in incorrect primary diagnosis, inappropriate medications, missing education/follow-up
LLM evaluators found 22% reduction in treatment errors, 19% in diagnostic errors
Effect became more pronounced after active clinician uptake strategies
Demonstrates potential for LLM-based CDS in real-world primary care settings
scale: 39,849 visits across Penda Health clinics in Nairobi, Kenya
